overview.argument: Revelation announces itself as the revelation of Jesus Christ — an unveiling, given to John, of the risen Lord and the end toward which all history moves. It is three things at once: an apocalypse (a symbolic vision of unseen realities), a prophecy (a summons to hear and obey, 1:3), and a letter to seven real congregations. After the opening vision of the glorified Christ (ch. 1) and his searching words to the seven churches (chs. 2–3), John is caught up to the throne room where God and the slain-yet-standing Lamb are worshiped (chs. 4–5). From there unfold three cycles of judgment — seven seals, seven trumpets, seven bowls — punctuated by visions of the dragon, the beasts, and Babylon, and of the Lamb's sealed people who conquer by his blood and their witness. The book ends where the whole Bible has been heading: the fall of Babylon, the marriage of the Lamb, the last judgment, and a new heaven and new earth where God dwells with his people (chs. 21–22).
overview.occasion: John writes from exile on the island of Patmos, for the word of God and the testimony of Jesus, most likely in the mid-90s AD under the emperor Domitian (some place it in the late 60s). His readers are seven churches in the Roman province of Asia, facing a mix of outright persecution, the seductions of the imperial cult and pagan trade guilds, and the subtler dangers of complacency and compromise (3:15–17). To a persecuted and pressured minority the book grants a heavenly vantage: the beast and Babylon look invincible, but the Lamb already reigns, and to "the one who conquers" — by faithful endurance, not by force — belongs the city to come.
overview.theology: Revelation is sustained worship and unflinching hope. Its center is the sovereignty of God over history and the Lamb who was slain and now shares the throne (5:6–14) — power redefined as sacrificial love, victory won through a cross. Around this turn its great themes: true worship over against idolatry and empire; faithful witness and the cost of martyrdom; the certainty of divine judgment on evil (the dragon, the two beasts, Babylon the harlot-city); the vindication of the saints; and the consummation, when heaven and earth are joined and every tear is wiped away (21:4). Its imagery is woven almost entirely from the Old Testament — Daniel, Ezekiel, Isaiah, Exodus, the Psalms — so that the whole canon's hope comes to a head in the Lamb.
overview.christ: No book displays Christ in more titles or greater glory. He is the faithful witness, the firstborn from the dead, the ruler of kings (1:5); the Alpha and the Omega, the first and the last (1:17; 22:13); the Son of Man among the lampstands, holding the churches in his hand (1:13–16); above all the Lamb who was slain, alone worthy to open the scroll, who by his blood ransomed people from every nation. He is the Lion of Judah and the Root of David (5:5), the rider called Faithful and True (19:11), the bridegroom of the new Jerusalem, and the one who says, Surely I am coming soon.

structure.label: Prologue and the vision of the risen Christ
structure.gist: The unveiling begins with the glorified Son of Man among the lampstands.

structure.label: The messages to the seven churches
structure.gist: Christ's searching praise, rebuke, and promise to each congregation.

structure.label: The throne, the scroll, and the Lamb
structure.gist: Heaven's worship of the Creator and of the slain Lamb who alone is worthy.

structure.label: The seven seals
structure.gist: Conquest, war, famine, death, the martyrs' cry, and the sealed servants of God.

structure.label: The seven trumpets
structure.gist: Escalating warnings, the little scroll, the two witnesses, the kingdom announced.

structure.label: The dragon, the beasts, and the Lamb's people
structure.gist: The cosmic conflict behind history; the mark of the beast; the Lamb on Zion.

structure.label: The seven bowls of wrath
structure.gist: The song of Moses and the Lamb, and the final plagues poured out on Babylon.

structure.label: The fall of Babylon and the rider on the white horse
structure.gist: The harlot-city judged, heaven's hallelujahs, the marriage supper, the Word of God victorious.

structure.label: The thousand years and the last judgment
structure.gist: Satan bound and loosed, the reign of the saints, the great white throne.

structure.label: New heaven, new earth, and epilogue
structure.gist: God dwelling with his people, the river of life, and "Come, Lord Jesus."

external.name: G.K. Beale
external.note: Reads Revelation as saturated with Old Testament allusion and structured by recapitulation rather than strict chronology.

external.name: Grant R. Osborne
external.note: A balanced verse-by-verse treatment attentive to the symbolic-visionary genre and its pastoral aim.

external.name: Richard Bauckham
external.work: The Theology of the Book of Revelation
external.note: Draws out the book's theology of worship, the Lamb's conquest by sacrifice, and witness as the church's calling.

external.name: Craig R. Koester
external.note: Sets the visions in their first-century Asian context of empire, commerce, and assembly worship.

external.name: Robert H. Mounce
external.work: The Book of Revelation (NICNT, rev. ed.)
external.note: A readable, historically grounded exposition holding judgment and hope together.

external.name: Ian Paul
external.note: Attends to the numerical and symbolic patterning and the book's call to faithful discipleship.
